---
title: "Modul 4 — Adopt-a-Gene (interactive)"
subtitle: "Bioinformatik NPLB24004U · runs in your browser, no install"
format:
  live-html:
    toc: true
    include-after-body: tutor.html
    resources:
      - data
engine: knitr
live:
  show-hints: true
  show-solutions: true
---

{{< include ./_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

# Welcome

This page **runs R live in your browser** (via webR) — nothing to install. You *adopt* a secondary-cell-wall gene and carry it through the analysis: **co-expression → network → enrichment → interpretation**, building a *gene dossier*.

**How it works**

- Edit the code where you see `______`, then press **Run** (▶). Cells share state, so run them **top to bottom**.
- Every code cell is **commented** — each line explains what it does and `# what each function does`, so you can follow the R even if a function is new to you.
- When a cell has a blank to fill, **running it prints ✅ if your answer is valid, or a hint** — no separate step.
- The heavy upstream steps (kallisto read-mapping, DESeq2) were **pre-computed** for you — this page loads the resulting tables **automatically when it opens**, so everything is instant. (A few setup cells are hidden to keep things tidy.)

> The expression matrix, protein-interaction edges and GO sets are **real** (Klepikova *Arabidopsis* atlas E-MTAB-7978, BioGRID, GO). The QC and differential-expression tables are small **illustrative examples** (the real ones come from the server pipeline — see the teacher guide).

---

```{webr}
#| autorun: true
#| echo: false
#| edit: false
#| output: false
# (hidden setup — runs automatically on page load)
# read.csv(path): read a comma-separated file into a data.frame (a table)
# check.names = FALSE keeps the organ column names exactly as written
m         <- read.csv("data/expression_matrix.csv", check.names = FALSE)  # genes × organ samples (TPM)
info      <- read.csv("data/gene_info.csv")        # gene_id, symbol, description
ppi       <- read.csv("data/ppi_edges.csv")        # protein–protein interactions (BioGRID)
gene_sets <- read.csv("data/gene_sets.csv")        # GO term → gene memberships
qc        <- read.csv("data/qc_table.csv")         # example kallisto QC
de        <- read.csv("data/de_results.csv")       # example DESeq2 result

organ_cols <- setdiff(names(m), "gene_id")          # every column except gene_id = the samples
EXPR <- as.matrix(m[, organ_cols])                  # numeric matrix (needed for cor()/apply())
rownames(EXPR) <- m$gene_id
desc <- setNames(info$description, info$gene_id)     # quick lookup: gene_id -> description
sym  <- setNames(info$symbol,      info$gene_id)
say  <- function(cond, good, bad)                   # tiny ✅/❌ feedback helper
  cat(if (isTRUE(cond)) paste("✅", good) else paste("❌", bad), "\n")

# draw_heatmap(): a small labelled heatmap (genes as rows, samples as columns)
draw_heatmap <- function(mat, main = ""){
  mat <- as.matrix(mat); ng <- nrow(mat); ns <- ncol(mat)
  pal <- hcl.colors(50, "YlGnBu", rev = TRUE)
  rng <- range(mat); norm <- (mat - rng[1]) / (diff(rng) + 1e-9)
  idx <- pmax(1, ceiling(norm * length(pal)))
  op <- par(mar = c(5, 6, 3, 1)); on.exit(par(op))
  plot(NA, xlim = c(0.5, ns + 0.5), ylim = c(0.5, ng + 0.5),
       axes = FALSE, xlab = "", ylab = "", main = main)
  for (i in 1:ng) for (j in 1:ns){
    yy <- ng - i + 1                                 # row 1 (Gene1) drawn at the top
    rect(j - 0.5, yy - 0.5, j + 0.5, yy + 0.5, col = pal[idx[i, j]], border = "white")
    text(j, yy, format(mat[i, j], trim = TRUE), cex = 0.65,
         col = if (norm[i, j] > 0.55) "white" else "black")
  }
  axis(1, at = 1:ns, labels = colnames(mat), las = 2, tick = FALSE, cex.axis = 0.8)
  axis(2, at = ng:1, labels = rownames(mat), las = 1, tick = FALSE, cex.axis = 0.8)
}

cat(sprintf("✅ Data loaded: %d genes × %d organ samples. You're ready.\n",
            nrow(EXPR), length(organ_cols)))
```

---

## Stage 0 — Claim your gene

Pick **one** gene from the pool (your TA prevents duplicates):

`CESA4 AT5G44030` · `CESA7 AT5G17420` · `CESA8 AT4G18780` · `IRX9 AT2G37090` · `LAC4 AT2G38080` · `MYB46 AT5G12870` · `NST1 AT1G32770` · `COBL4 AT5G15630` · decoys `AT1G19300`, `AT3G16920`

**Instructions:** replace both `______`, then **Run** — the cell tells you if your gene is valid.

```{webr}
MY_GENE <- "______"   # <- your gene's AGI locus, e.g. "AT5G17420"
MY_NAME <- "______"   # <- a short name, e.g. "CESA7"
cat("Adopted:", MY_NAME, "(", MY_GENE, ")\n")   # cat() prints its arguments

# instant check: grepl() tests the AGI text pattern; %in% checks the gene is actually in the data
say(grepl("^AT[1-5CM]G[0-9]{5}$", MY_GENE) && MY_GENE %in% rownames(EXPR),
    "valid gene, present in the data — onwards!",
    "not a valid/known locus — pick one from the pool above and re-run.")
```

<details><summary>💡 Hint</summary>
An AGI locus looks like `AT` + chromosome (1–5) + `G` + five digits, in quotes: `"AT5G17420"`.
</details>

**✍️ Dossier:** write your hypothesis — *genes co-expressed with my gene are enriched for ______, so my gene acts in ______.*

---

## Stage 1 — Look at the data: heatmaps, outliers & normalization

Real count data is messy — before any analysis, **look at it**. Here is a small *Arabidopsis* count matrix: 12 genes across root, leaf and pollen samples. **Run it** to see the heatmap.

```{webr}
genes   <- paste0("Gene", 1:12)
samples <- c("Root1","Root2","Root3","Leaf1","Leaf2","Leaf3","Pollen1")
# matrix(values, byrow = TRUE): fill row by row; dimnames = list(row labels, column labels)
counts <- matrix(c(
   80,    85,  78,   2,   1,   3,    0,
   90,   100,  88,   5,   2,   4,    0,
    2,     1,   3,  70,  75,  80,    0,
    3,     2,   4,  85,  82,  90,    0,
    0,     1,   0,   2,   1,   0,  120,
   20,    20,  20,  20,  20,  20,   20,
    0,     0,   1,   0,   0,   0,    0,
    5, 100000,  6,   7,   5,   6,    8,
   10,    15,  12,  25,  30,  28,    5,
   40,    38,  42,  45,  47,  50,   41,
    5,     9,   6,  12,  15,  11,    4,
    3,     5,   4,  30,  28,  26,   10),
  nrow = 12, byrow = TRUE, dimnames = list(genes, samples))

draw_heatmap(counts, "Raw counts")     # labelled heatmap (helper is pre-defined for you)
```

> **Look closely:** can you see any tissue pattern? You can't — **one cell, `Gene8` in `Root2` = 100000, swamps the entire colour scale.** That's a technical **outlier**, and it also makes `Root2` look like a giant sample. Two things to deal with: outliers and differing sequencing depth.

### Step 1.1 — Find the outlier

```{webr}
# which(x == max(x), arr.ind = TRUE): the [row, column] position of the largest value
hit <- which(counts == max(counts), arr.ind = TRUE)
cat("Largest value:", max(counts), "→", rownames(counts)[hit[1]], "in", colnames(counts)[hit[2]], "\n")

say(rownames(counts)[hit[1]] == "Gene8" && colnames(counts)[hit[2]] == "Root2",
    "yes — Gene8 in Root2 is the outlier (a spike that also inflates that whole sample).",
    "re-run the matrix cell above first.")
```

### Step 1.2 — Tame it with a log transform

Huge values hide everything else. **`log2(x + 1)`** compresses the scale so patterns appear (the `+ 1` avoids `log2(0)`). **Fill the blank with `1`**, then Run and compare the heatmap.

```{webr}
logc <- log2(counts + ______)          # <- the +1 keeps zeros finite: log2(0 + 1) = 0
draw_heatmap(logc, "log2(counts + 1)")

say(exists("logc") && is.finite(min(logc)) && abs(min(logc)) < 1e-9,
    "now the biology shows: Gene1–2 root-high, Gene3–4 leaf-high, Gene5 pollen-specific, Gene6 flat.",
    "use + 1 so log2(0 + 1) = 0 (no -Inf), then re-run.")
```

<details><summary>💡 Hint</summary>
A log of zero is <code>-Inf</code>. Adding 1 first makes the smallest value <code>log2(1) = 0</code>.
</details>

### Step 1.3 — Normalize for sequencing depth

Logging helps you *see*. To *compare a gene across samples* of different sequencing depth, normalize so every sample sums to the same total (here, one million — "counts per million"). **Fill the blank with `colSums(counts)`.**

```{webr}
# sweep(x, 2, v, "/"): divide each COLUMN (margin 2) of x by the matching value in v
cpm <- sweep(counts, 2, ______, "/") * 1e6
cat("Sample totals after normalization (should all be 1,000,000):\n"); print(colSums(cpm))

say(all(abs(colSums(cpm) - 1e6) < 1),
    "every sample now sums to 1,000,000 — comparable across depths. (TPM additionally divides by gene length.)",
    "divide by each column's own total: colSums(counts).")
```

> **Rule for your dossier:** *estimated counts → differential expression; normalized values (TPM/CPM) → comparing a gene across samples.* Never DE-test normalized values.

---

## Stage 2 — QC: which sample do we drop?

Pre-computed kallisto QC (example). A bad library poisons everything downstream.

```{webr}
#| autorun: true
qc          # typing an object's name just prints it (here, the QC table)
```

**Instructions:** find the failing sample (lowest % pseudoaligned).

```{webr}
# which.min(v): the POSITION of the smallest value in v.  qc$col -> the column named col.
# So this picks the sample row with the lowest pseudoalignment %.
worst <- qc$sample[which.min(qc$pct_pseudoaligned)]
cat("Lowest pseudoalignment:", worst, "\n")
# min(): the smallest value.  paste(): glue text together.
say(min(qc$pct_pseudoaligned) < 50,
    paste("drop", worst, "before building the matrix — a bad library distorts PCA & co-expression"),
    "look again at pct_pseudoaligned")
```

> **Rule for your dossier:** *estimated counts → differential expression; TPM → comparing a gene across samples.* Never DE-test TPM.

---

## Stage 2b — Exploratory data analysis: PCA & clustering  *(synthetic demo)*

Before analysing real data you **explore its structure**: do samples group the way biology predicts? Any outliers or **batch effects**? We use a small **synthetic** dataset — 3 tissues × 4 replicates, with a deliberate batch effect — so the patterns are crystal clear. Just run the cells and read the plots.

```{webr}
#| autorun: true
set.seed(1)                                       # fix the random seed so results are reproducible
n_genes <- 300
tissue  <- rep(c("leaf","root","seed"), each = 4) # rep(): repeat -> 12 samples, 3 tissues × 4 reps
batch   <- rep(c("A","B"), times = 6)             # two sequencing batches, interleaved

# rnorm(n, mean, sd): n random numbers from a normal distribution.
# matrix(...): a distinct "true" expression profile per tissue (rows = genes, cols = tissues)
profile <- matrix(rnorm(n_genes * 3, mean = 6, sd = 2), nrow = n_genes,
                  dimnames = list(NULL, c("leaf","root","seed")))

# sapply(seq, f): apply f to each sample index and bind the results into a matrix (genes × samples).
# each sample = its tissue's profile + measurement noise (+ a small shift for batch B)
X <- sapply(seq_along(tissue), function(i)
       profile[, tissue[i]] + rnorm(n_genes, 0, 0.7) + ifelse(batch[i] == "B", 0.6, 0))
colnames(X) <- paste0(tissue, "_", batch, seq_along(tissue))   # paste0(): glue text with no separator
cat("Synthetic data:", nrow(X), "genes ×", ncol(X), "samples\n")
```

### PCA — do samples separate by tissue?

PCA squeezes the 300 genes into a few **principal components** that capture the most variation between samples.

```{webr}
#| autorun: true
# prcomp(x, scale. = TRUE): principal component analysis (scaled). t() transposes so SAMPLES are rows.
pca <- prcomp(t(X), scale. = TRUE)
# pca$x = sample coordinates; [,1] and [,2] are PC1 and PC2.
# factor(tissue): turn labels into colour groups; pch = 19: filled dots; cex: point size
plot(pca$x[,1], pca$x[,2], col = factor(tissue), pch = 19, cex = 1.4,
     xlab = "PC1", ylab = "PC2", main = "PCA — coloured by tissue")
text(pca$x[,1], pca$x[,2], colnames(X), pos = 3, cex = 0.6)        # text(): label each point
legend("topright", legend = levels(factor(tissue)), col = 1:3, pch = 19)
```

How much variation does each component capture? (a **scree** plot)

```{webr}
#| autorun: true
# pca$sdev = standard deviation of each PC; squared = variance. Divide by the total for a proportion.
var_explained <- pca$sdev^2 / sum(pca$sdev^2)
barplot(100 * var_explained[1:6], names.arg = paste0("PC", 1:6),
        ylab = "% variance explained", main = "Scree plot")
```

### Hierarchical clustering — which samples are most alike?

```{webr}
#| autorun: true
# dist(): all pairwise distances between samples (rows). hclust(): build a tree by repeatedly
# joining the two closest groups (ward.D2 = a common linkage rule).
d  <- dist(t(X))
hc <- hclust(d, method = "ward.D2")
plot(hc, labels = colnames(X), main = "Hierarchical clustering of samples", xlab = "", sub = "")
```

**🔮 Your turn:** tissue should dominate, with the A/B batch effect minor. **Fill the blank** with `batch` to recolour the PCA by sequencing batch — running it confirms PCA ran and judges whether batch matters here.

```{webr}
plot(pca$x[,1], pca$x[,2], col = factor(______), pch = 19, cex = 1.4,   # <- replace ______ with batch
     xlab = "PC1", ylab = "PC2", main = "PCA — coloured by batch")
legend("topright", legend = levels(factor(batch)), col = 1:2, pch = 19)

say(exists("pca"),
    "PCA + clustering done — the three tissues form clear clusters; the batch effect is small here.",
    "run the synthetic-data and PCA cells above first.")
```

> In **real** data, if samples cluster by **batch** instead of biology, you correct for it (e.g. add batch to a DESeq2 design, or ComBat) **before** drawing conclusions.

---

## Stage 3 — Co-expression of YOUR gene  *(the heart of the dossier)*

**Guilt by association:** genes switched on/off together often work together. Correlate every gene with **your** gene. **Fill the blank** with `target`, then **Run** — the check confirms your own gene tops the list (PCC = 1.000).

```{webr}
# EXPR[MY_GENE, ]  -> pull ONE row: your gene's expression across all 56 organs
target <- EXPR[MY_GENE, ]

# apply(X, 1, f): run function f on each ROW of matrix X   (margin 1 = rows, 2 = columns)
# function(r) ...: r is one gene's expression vector (a single row)
# cor(a, b): Pearson correlation coefficient (PCC) between two numeric vectors (−1 … +1)
# suppressWarnings(): hide the harmless warning for genes with no variation (standard deviation = 0)
pcc <- apply(EXPR, 1, function(r) suppressWarnings(cor(r, ______)))

# data.frame(): assemble a results table.  round(x, 3): keep 3 decimals
result <- data.frame(gene        = rownames(EXPR),
                     symbol      = sym[rownames(EXPR)],
                     description = desc[rownames(EXPR)],
                     PCC         = round(pcc, 3))
# order(-result$PCC): row order sorted by PCC, highest first.  head(x, 100): keep the top 100
result <- head(result[order(-result$PCC), ], 100)
print(head(result, 12))                            # show the top 12 neighbours

# instant check: row 1 should be your own gene, correlated 1.000 with itself
say(nrow(result) > 0 && result$gene[1] == MY_GENE && round(result$PCC[1], 3) == 1,
    paste("row 1 is", MY_GENE, "with PCC = 1.000 — your neighbour list is correct."),
    "row 1 should be your own gene with PCC = 1.000 — check Stage 0 and the blank above.")
```

<details><summary>💡 Hint</summary>
<code>target</code> holds your gene's expression across all 56 organs — correlate each gene against it.
</details>

**✍️ Dossier:** from the neighbours' descriptions, *before any lookup*, predict your gene's function.

---

## Stage 4 — "Why 0.7 is a guess": choose a threshold

We build the all-vs-all correlation among your 100 neighbours and count edges at several cutoffs. Run it, then decide.

```{webr}
nb  <- result$gene          # the 100 neighbour gene ids
sub <- EXPR[nb, ]           # their expression rows only
# t(): transpose (swap rows/columns) so cor() correlates GENES against each other, not samples.
# cor(matrix): returns the full gene × gene correlation matrix.
C   <- cor(t(sub))
# upper.tri(C): a TRUE/FALSE mask of the upper triangle, so each gene-pair is counted once.
ut  <- upper.tri(C)
known <- c("AT5G44030","AT5G17420","AT4G18780","AT2G37090",
           "AT2G38080","AT5G12870","AT1G32770","AT5G15630")   # known cell-wall genes
# for: repeat the block for each cutoff value
for (cut in c(0.5, 0.7, 0.9)) {
  keep  <- ut & C > cut                              # keep edges above the cutoff (and in the upper triangle)
  # row(C)/col(C): the row/column index of every cell; [keep] picks the kept edges' genes
  nodes <- unique(c(rownames(C)[row(C)[keep]], colnames(C)[col(C)[keep]]))  # unique() removes duplicates
  # sum(keep): how many edges (TRUEs).  %in%: which known genes survived
  cat(sprintf("cutoff %.1f → %5d edges, %4d genes, %d known cell-wall genes kept\n",
              cut, sum(keep), length(nodes), sum(known %in% nodes)))
}
```

> Pick the cutoff that keeps a clean module **without** dropping known partners, and justify it in your dossier. *(WGCNA's soft-thresholding automates exactly this choice.)*

---

## Stage 5 — Build the network (+ protein interactions)

Choose your cutoff, then overlay **physical** interactions from BioGRID. **Fill the blank** with your cutoff (e.g. `0.8`) and **Run** — it reports the edges and your top hubs.

```{webr}
MY_CUTOFF <- ______          # the cutoff you justified, e.g. 0.8
keep  <- ut & C > MY_CUTOFF   # the co-expression edges to keep
# build an edge table (source gene, target gene, edge type) for the kept pairs
edges <- data.frame(source = rownames(C)[row(C)[keep]],
                    target = colnames(C)[col(C)[keep]], type = "coexpression")

# ppi[ rows , cols ]: filter the PPI table to interactions where BOTH genes are neighbours (%in% nb)
pp <- ppi[ppi$source %in% nb & ppi$target %in% nb, c("source","target")]
# rbind(): stack rows -> add the PPI edges underneath the co-expression edges
if (nrow(pp) > 0) { pp$type <- "PPI"; edges <- rbind(edges, pp) }

# table(): count how many times each gene appears = its degree (number of edges).
# sort(decreasing = TRUE): biggest first -> the hubs.
deg <- sort(table(c(edges$source, edges$target)), decreasing = TRUE)
cat("Edges:", nrow(edges), " (PPI:", sum(edges$type == "PPI"), ")\n\nTop hubs (by degree):\n")
print(head(deg, 6))                                  # the 6 most-connected genes

# instant check: did you build a non-empty network?
say(exists("edges") && nrow(edges) > 0,
    "network built — note your top hub and whether it's a known regulator (MYB/NAC).",
    "no edges — lower MY_CUTOFF a little and re-run.")
```

> For a publication figure, export `edges` and open it in **Cytoscape** (Network Analyzer for betweenness). To download: run `write.csv(edges, "edges.csv")` then use your browser's file panel.

---

## Stage 6 — Functional enrichment (GO over-representation)

Is your neighbourhood enriched for a biological process? We test each GO term with the hypergeometric test (all base R — no packages).

```{webr}
bg   <- rownames(EXPR); N <- length(bg)              # background = all genes in the matrix; N = how many
hits <- result$gene                                  # our "interesting" list = the co-expression neighbours
gs   <- gene_sets[gene_sets$gene_id %in% bg, ]       # keep only GO memberships for background genes
# split(values, groups): make a list, one entry per GO term, holding that term's genes
terms <- split(gs$gene_id, gs$term_id)
# tapply(values, groups, fun): apply fun within each group -> here, grab each term's readable name
tname <- tapply(gs$term_name, gs$term_id, function(x) x[1])

# lapply(items, f): run f on each item, returning a list.  do.call(rbind, list): stack into one data.frame
ora <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(terms), function(t) {
  K <- length(unique(terms[[t]]))                    # genes in this term (within background)
  k <- sum(hits %in% terms[[t]])                     # how many of OUR hits fall in this term
  if (k < 2) return(NULL)                            # skip tiny overlaps (return nothing)
  # phyper(k-1, K, N-K, n, lower.tail = FALSE): hypergeometric p-value = chance of ≥k hits by luck
  data.frame(term = t, name = tname[[t]], k = k, K = K,
             p = phyper(k - 1, K, N - K, length(hits), lower.tail = FALSE))
}))
ora$padj <- p.adjust(ora$p, "BH")                    # p.adjust(): correct p-values for many tests (BH = FDR)
ora <- head(ora[order(ora$p), c("name","k","K","padj")], 12)   # most significant terms first
print(ora)

# instant check: did enrichment return any terms?
say(exists("ora") && nrow(ora) > 0,
    paste0("top enriched term: ", ora$name[1], " — does it match your prediction?"),
    "no terms returned — make sure Stage 3 ran first.")
```

---

## Stage 7 — Differential expression to interpret  *(precomputed example)*

A volcano from a pre-computed DESeq2-style result (example data). Red = significant (padj < 0.05).

```{webr}
#| autorun: true
# plot(x, y): scatter plot.  pch = 20: small filled dots.
# -log10(padj): transforms p-values so SMALL p = HIGH on the plot (more significant = taller).
# ifelse(cond, a, b): for each gene, choose colour a if significant, else b (vectorised if/else).
plot(de$log2FoldChange, -log10(de$padj + 1e-300),
     pch = 20, col = ifelse(de$padj < 0.05, "firebrick", "grey70"),
     xlab = "log2 fold change", ylab = "-log10 adjusted p",
     main = "Volcano (precomputed example DESeq2 result)")
abline(h = -log10(0.05), lty = 2)                    # abline(h=): a horizontal line at the p = 0.05 threshold
# sum(condition): counts how many genes are TRUE (significant AND up-regulated)
cat("Up-regulated (padj<0.05 & log2FC>1):", sum(de$padj < 0.05 & de$log2FoldChange > 1), "\n")
```

> Why a model (DESeq2) not a t-test on TPM? With few replicates and ~20,000 genes, naive testing yields thousands of false hits; DESeq2 shrinks fold-changes and controls the false-discovery rate. *(Run the real DESeq2 on a replicated dataset using the server notebook.)*

---

## Stage 8 — Close the dossier  *(assessed)*

Write a one-page interpretation, with evidence from *your* analyses:

1. **Enriched process** in your gene's neighbourhood? *(Stage 6)*
2. **Top hub** gene — a known regulator (NAC/MYB)? *(Stage 5)*
3. **Verdict:** does the evidence **confirm or revise** your Stage-0 hypothesis? Name the evidence.

> **Graded (4 × 25%):** reproducible work · network-figure quality · correct enrichment interpretation · hypothesis revisited with named evidence.

*Built on the course's real kallisto + E-MTAB-7978 + BioGRID + GO pipeline; heavy steps pre-computed so the analysis runs in your browser.*
